sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Carbon monoxide (CO) is a poisonous, colourless, odourless, and tasteless gas produced by incomplete combustion of fuels such as gas, wood, coal, and, in some cases, plastics. CO binds to haemoglobin, reducing oxygen delivery to tissues and impairing cellular respiration, which causes tissue hypoxia. Common hazardous sources include contained fires, car exhausts, petrol-powered machinery, industrial exposure, and confined spaces with poor ventilation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, confusion, dizziness, syncope
- Tachycardia, palpitations, arrythmia, hypotension, myocardial ischemia
- Pulmonary oedema, dyspnoea
- Nausea/vomiting
- Fatigue, malaise, ataxia
- Seizure, coma, death

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Gas detection must take place before entering any area suspected of containing a hazardous atmosphere
- Investigating why/how the exposure occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- The classic red or pink appearance is uncommon and should not be relied on diagnostically
- Standard pulse oximetry can be misleading in CO poisoning and does not exclude significant exposure
- Patients with cardiovascular disease, pregnancy, reduced consciousness, or prolonged exposure are at higher risk of serious toxicity
- Delayed neuropsychiatric symptoms can occur after apparent initial recovery, so maintain a low threshold for suspicion and transport

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey with responder safety prioritised
- Remove the patient from the hazardous atmosphere as soon as it is safe to do so
- Administer high-concentration oxygen via non-rebreather mask as soon as possible
- Manage airway if required
- Record vital signs and remember standard pulse oximetry may appear falsely reassuring
- Consider multiple-patient exposure, scene source control, and need for wider notification
- Complete focused secondary survey and expedite transport

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway if required
- Obtain 12 lead ECG
- Gain IV access if clinically indicated
- Consider blood testing, including carboxyhaemoglobin where available, and troponin if cardiac symptoms are present
- Consider antiemetic therapy if required
- Transport all suspected significant exposures to hospital for definitive assessment

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Up to 40% may develop delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS) days to weeks after exposure — memory loss, personality change, movement disorders, depression
- Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy is the definitive treatment for severe CO poisoning (loss of consciousness, seizure, cardiac compromise, pregnancy, or persistent symptoms). Confirm the nearest HBO facility and transport pathway for any severe case
- In fire or smoke inhalation victims who are critically ill (coma, seizure, or haemodynamic compromise with metabolic acidosis and elevated lactate), concurrent cyanide poisoning should be considered and empirically treated with hydroxocobalamin — this is common in structural fires and industrial combustion events
